clean_story:
Shared words. Faster trust.

The phrase language as bridge fuel describes how a common tongue acts like kindling—igniting cooperation where silence or misunderstanding would stall it. Not a permanent structure, but the spark that lets strangers build one.

A construction site in Dubai: 20 nationalities, 12 languages. The foreman shouts "Lift!" in broken Hindi-English-Arabic hybrid. Hands move in sync. No time for translation apps; the shared fragments of Urdu loanwords and English numbers ("ek, do, teen—GO!") cut delay from minutes to seconds. Studies show teams with even a 10-word overlap complete tasks 37% faster than those relying on gestures alone.

Or consider the 1970s lingua franca of West African markets: a pidgin mixing English, Yoruba, and Twi. Traders invented it on the fly, stripping grammar to verbs and nouns ("Me want yam—how much?"). No dictionaries. No classes. Just the minimum fuel to swap goods, then dissolve. Linguists call this "transient mutualism"—language as a tool borrowed, not owned.

Military radios use brevity codes ("Bingo fuel" = "I’m almost empty") for the same reason: clarity under pressure. Every extra syllable risks misheard orders. Bridge fuel isn’t about poetry. It’s about compression.

Three related subjects are trade pidgins history, emergency communication codes, and how silence builds walls.
